text: The surgeon uses three ports on the left side, including a submemory incision and one in the axilla, to access the anterior mediastinum. The mediastinal pleura is opened anterior to the left phrenic nerve, and the lump on the left thymic lobe is lifted inferiorly. The pleura of the fibrous pericardium is opened to release the mass, followed by dissection underneath to lift it off the pulmonary trunk and the ascending and arch of the aorta. As the dissection progresses upwards, the blood vessels draining into and out of the left internal mammary are carefully dissected, ensuring the left phrenic nerve is not injured.

The surgeon retracts the left thymic horn extending into the neck, following it up to the thyroid. Using a hook and bipolar fenestrated instrument, the surgeon clips the vessels at the top of the thymic horn before dividing them with diathermy.

The surgeon releases the right thymus horn and approaches the confluence of the innominate veins into the superior vena cava (SVC). Using a hemlock, the vein draining into the SVC is isolated. Blunt dissection is employed to avoid injuring the right phrenic nerve, which runs from the right internal mammary base to the lateral side of the SVC. A lymph node is visible, and the surgeon uses bipolar cautery to safely divide tissue without conducting electricity to the phrenic nerve.

The surgeon opens the right pleura to enhance visualization, then divides the remaining tissue. An endocatch bag is used to contain the specimen, inserted through a 12mm port in the axilla. The bag is closed and extracted, revealing the innominate vein and aorta clear of tissue. A comparative view shows the area before and after tumor resection.
